title: TSA Hands Security to Private Firms at Tampa, Des Moines
excerpt: TSA's new Gold+ program lets private contractors run screening at Tampa and Des Moines airports. Here's what actually changes for travelers and what doesn't.
content:
The Transportation Security Administration has picked its first two test cases for a bigger experiment: letting private companies run airport security screening from top to bottom. Tampa International Airport and Des Moines International Airport have both opted into a new program called TSA Gold+, under which private contractors will hire and manage the screening workforce and control checkpoint technology, while TSA steps back into a purely regulatory and oversight role. TSA released a revised request for proposals on July 17, with bids from prospective contractors due July 31, and the resulting contracts are expected to run for ten years. Tampa has said its transition is targeted for May 31, 2027, so this is not an overnight change, but it is a real shift in how two major airports will run security for the next decade.

If you're flying through Tampa or Des Moines any time soon, the honest answer is: nothing changes at the checkpoint yet, and when it eventually does, the rules you follow shouldn't change either. Gold+ builds on an existing arrangement called the Screening Partnership Program, which already covers about 20 US airports, including San Francisco International, Kansas City International, and Orlando Sanford, and travelers there go through the same 3-1-1 liquids rules, the same ID checks, and the same TSA PreCheck and CLEAR lanes as anywhere else. TSA sets the security standards and audits compliance regardless of who is badly the checkpoint staff; the contractor just becomes the employer. So a traveler's actual experience, in theory, should look the same: same lines, same rules, same REAL ID and ConfirmID requirements that took effect earlier this year, just a different name on the paycheck of the person checking your boarding pass.

Where this does matter for travelers is less about the walk through the metal detector and more about airport resilience. Tampa's airport leadership specifically framed the move around reducing the risk that a federal government shutdown or funding lapse disrupts screening operations, since privately employed screeners wouldn't be furloughed the way federal TSA officers have been in past shutdowns. That's worth remembering the next time a budget standoff in Washington makes headlines close to a travel date: airports operating under this newer model may be somewhat more insulated from the kind of screening slowdowns that hit federally staffed checkpoints during the 2018-2019 shutdown. On the other side of the debate, the union representing roughly 47,000 TSA officers nationwide has pushed back hard, arguing the change is about contractor profit rather than passenger safety, and workforce advocates are watching closely as more airports weigh whether to apply.

For a traveler booking flights right now, there's genuinely no action required. Nothing about ID requirements, liquids limits, PreCheck enrollment, or what you can pack in a carry-on is changing because of this announcement. If you already have TSA PreCheck or Global Entry, keep using it exactly as before at both airports. If you're flying out of Tampa between now and mid-2027, expect the checkpoint experience to look and feel the same throughout the transition, since the contractor selection process alone runs past this summer's proposal deadline before any operational changeover even begins. The bigger story to watch is whether other mid-size airports follow Tampa and Des Moines into Gold+ over the coming months; if a hub you fly through regularly announces a similar move, the practical takeaway will likely stay the same, standard TSA rules still apply, but it's worth checking that airport's own announcements for any pilot changes to checkpoint technology, like new scanning equipment, that a private operator might roll out ahead of other airports.
